items.myth: The most complex model you can train is always best if you have enough compute.
items.reality: Validation error usually rises after a point — extra complexity fits noise and increases variance. Compute does not remove the U-curve on finite data.
items.example: Depth-30 tree on 200 rows: perfect train fit, worse val MSE than depth-6 despite more CPU time.

items.myth: Model complexity and number of parameters are the same thing.
items.reality: Effective complexity depends on regularization, early stopping, feature count, and data size — a regularized wide net may generalize like a smaller one.
items.example: Same 1M-parameter net: early stop at epoch 5 vs train to epoch 500 — vastly different val accuracy.

items.myth: There is one universal 'correct' complexity for a given algorithm.
items.reality: The sweet spot depends on dataset size, noise, and true function smoothness — optimal depth for 500 rows differs from 500k rows.
items.example: kNN with k=1 wins on 10k clean points; k=1 fails on 200 noisy points where k=15 is best.

items.myth: If validation error is high, you should always increase model complexity.
items.reality: High val error with low train error means overfitting — reduce complexity. Increase complexity only when both train and val error are high (underfitting).
items.example: Train MSE 0.05, val MSE 0.80 on high-degree poly — lower degree, do not raise it.
